steps.message:
# Task: Generate Flipt Release Notes

**Repository:** flipt-io/flipt
**Output:** Write your release notes to `/workspace/RELEASE_NOTES.md`

## Objective

Generate comprehensive release notes for Flipt summarizing API changes. Flipt is an open-source feature flag management system with REST and gRPC APIs. Analyze the codebase to identify what changed in terms of the public API surface.

## Scope

Analyze the following areas for API changes:
- `rpc/flipt/` — protobuf API definitions (gRPC surface)
- `internal/server/` — REST API handlers
- `internal/storage/` — storage backend changes
- `ui/` — frontend changes if any significant ones exist
- `CHANGELOG.md` in the repository (use as reference, not as copy)

## Output Format

Write to `/workspace/RELEASE_NOTES.md`:

```markdown
# Flipt Release Notes

## Breaking Changes

> These changes require action from users upgrading.

- **[API/Storage/Config]**: Description + migration path

## New Features

- **[component]**: Description with API example if applicable

## Deprecations

- **[component]**: What is deprecated, what to use instead, when it will be removed

## Bug Fixes

- **[component]**: Description

## Upgrade Guide

Step-by-step instructions for users upgrading from the previous version.
```

## Quality Bar

- Every breaking change must include a migration path
- Every deprecation must specify the replacement
- New features must reference the specific API endpoint or config option
- The upgrade guide must be actionable (numbered steps)

## Anti-Requirements

- Do not copy the existing CHANGELOG.md
- Do not fabricate API endpoints — verify in rpc/ or internal/server/
I'll help you generate comprehensive release notes for Flipt by analyzing the codebase. Let me start by exploring the repository structure and understanding the recent changes.

todos.content: Explore codebase structure and find recent commits
Analyze protobuf API definitions in rpc/flipt/
Review REST API handlers in internal/server/
Check storage backend changes in internal/storage/
Review CHANGELOG.md for context
Generate release notes document

file.content:
# Changelog

This format is based on [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.0.0/)
and this project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/spec/v2.0.0.html).

## [v1.40.1](https://github.com/flipt-io/flipt/releases/tag/v1.40.1) - 2024-04-11

### Changed

- add halcyonCorsair as a contributor for code (#2970)

### Fixed

- only store auth hash in cache (#2974)
- `auth`: skip establishing a database connection when not necessary (#2973)
- `cache`: accept a username in redis configuration (#2953)

## [v1.40.0](https://github.com/flipt-io/flipt/releases/tag/v1.40.0) - 2024-04-05

### Added

- `oci`: better integration OCI storage with AWS ECR (#2941)
- `fs/git`: add support for a directory scope (#2939)
- `cmd`: allow passing a working directory for flipt validate command (#2936)

### Changed

- moved validation into its separate package (#2831)

### Fixed

- invalid trace log level schema (#2946)
- jsonschema to correctly validate key|keys in rollout segment (#2947)
- `ui`: evaluation console provides the warning for user about context values (#2937)
- dont error on parsing in evaluator (#2918)

## [v1.39.2](https://github.com/flipt-io/flipt/releases/tag/v1.39.2) - 2024-03-29

### Fixed

- isnotoneof operator using incorrect logic (#2912)
- issue with segment constraints if belonging to same segment key in different namespaces (#2920)

## [v1.39.1](https://github.com/flipt-io/flipt/releases/tag/v1.39.1) - 2024-03-27

### Added

- make oci manifest version configurable (#2908)

## [v1.39.0](https://github.com/flipt-io/flipt/releases/tag/v1.39.0) - 2024-03-25

### Added

- add team membership check to GitHub authentication method (#2891)
- clear session if authentication methods change
- add command bar (#2815)
- add JWT aware UI and revise metadata for audit events

### Changed

- `ui`: assistance for arrays data in constraints (#2889)

### Fixed

- `ui`: allow dot in the key of variant (#2887)
- missing slash in copy as curl (#2875)
- `ui`: prevent react warning (#2845)
- `ui`: show placeholder for entity id oneof* constraint (#2799)

## [v1.38.2](https://github.com/flipt-io/flipt/releases/tag/v1.38.2) - 2024-03-15

### Fixed

- shadow bug for segments in Flipt Data API (#2860)

## [v1.38.1](https://github.com/flipt-io/flipt/releases/tag/v1.38.1) - 2024-03-13

### Fixed

- Analytics page returning 501 if not enabled (#2486)

## [v1.38.0](https://github.com/flipt-io/flipt/releases/tag/v1.38.0) - 2024-02-23

### Added

- add ability to check known flipt server verison (#2791)
- add integration test case for entity id adding
- `flipt`: add entityId as a constraint type and operator
- `otlp`: account for service name in environment variable (#2775)

### Changed

- `tracing`: move tracing setup into own package and extra tests added (#2776)

### Fixed

- `ui`: stop removing upper case letters in keys (#2790)
- `analytics`: aggregate data to run queries faster (#2778)
- `ui`: prevent multiple analytics requests at once with polling (#2777)

### Added

- add integration test case for entity id adding
- `flipt`: add entityId as a constraint type and operator
- `otlp`: account for service name in environment variable (#2775)

### Changed

- `tracing`: move tracing setup into own package and extra tests added (#2776)

### Fixed

- `ui`: stop removing upper case letters in keys (#2790)

file.content:
# Release

This document describes the release process for Flipt.

The release process is managed by the [Release GitHub Action](.github/workflows/release.yml) and is triggered by pushing a tag to the repository that matches the pattern `v*` (e.g. `v1.0.0`).

Flipt is currently released as a single binary for Linux (arm64 and amd64) and macOS (arm64). The release process builds the binary for each platform and uploads them to the [Flipt GitHub Releases page](https://github.com/flipt-io/flipt/releases).

We also publish a Docker image for each release to [Docker Hub](https://hub.docker.com/r/flipt/flipt/tags) and [GitHub Container Registry](https://github.com/flipt-io/flipt/pkgs/container/flipt).

## Release Process

We use GitHub Actions and [GoReleaser Pro](https://goreleaser.com/) to build and publish Flipt releases.

We support three main types of releases:

- Stable releases (e.g. `v1.0.0`)
- Snapshot releases (e.g. `v1.0.0-snapshot`)
- Nightly releases (e.g. `v1.0.0-nightly`)

The process for each looks roughly the same using GitHub Actions and GoReleaser Pro, but there are some differences in how the release is tagged and how the release is published.

![Release Process](.github/images/release-process.png)

### Stable Releases

1. Create a new branch from `main` named `release/vX.Y.Z` (e.g. `release/v1.0.0`).
2. Update the CHANGELOG.md with the release notes for the new version. (We have a `mage` task to help with this in the `build` directory.)
3. Commit the changes and push the branch to GitHub.
4. Create a pull request from the release branch to `main`.
5. Once the pull request is merged, create a new tag on `main` with the version number (e.g. `v1.0.0`).
6. Push the tag to GitHub.
7. CI will build and publish the release to GitHub and Docker Hub.

### Snapshot Releases

Snapshot releases are created on-demand and are used to test the build and release process for stable releases.

To create a snapshot release, we run the [Snapshot Release GitHub Action](.github/workflows/snapshot.yml) workflow manually from the GitHub Actions UI.

The snapshot release does not create an actual release, it simply builds the binaries and uploads them as artifacts to the workflow run. The artifacts can then be downloaded and tested.

### Nightly Releases

Nightly releases are created automatically every night at 23:30 UTC via the [Nightly Release GitHub Action](.github/workflows/nightly.yml).

The nightly release also does not create an actual release. Unlike the snapshot release, the nightly release does not upload the binaries as artifacts. Instead, the nightly release builds a Docker image and pushes it to Docker Hub and GitHub Container Registry (Linux arm and x64 only). The Docker image is tagged with the version number (e.g. `v1.0.0-nightly`).

Currently the nightly release only builds the Docker image for Linux. We do not build a nightly release for macOS.

# Release

This document describes the release process for Flipt.

The release process is managed by the [Release GitHub Action](.github/workflows/release.yml) and is triggered by pushing a tag to the repository that matches the pattern `v*` (e.g. `v1.0.0`).

Flipt is currently released as a single binary for Linux (arm64 and amd64) and macOS (arm64). The release process builds the binary for each platform and uploads them to the [Flipt GitHub Releases page](https://github.com/flipt-io/flipt/releases).

We also publish a Docker image for each release to [Docker Hub](https://hub.docker.com/r/flipt/flipt/tags) and [GitHub Container Registry](https://github.com/flipt-io/flipt/pkgs/container/flipt).

## Release Process

We use GitHub Actions and [GoReleaser Pro](https://goreleaser.com/) to build and publish Flipt releases.

We support three main types of releases:

- Stable releases (e.g. `v1.0.0`)
- Snapshot releases (e.g. `v1.0.0-snapshot`)
- Nightly releases (e.g. `v1.0.0-nightly`)

The process for each looks roughly the same using GitHub Actions and GoReleaser Pro, but there are some differences in how the release is tagged and how the release is published.

![Release Process](.github/images/release-process.png)

### Stable Releases

1. Create a new branch from `main` named `release/vX.Y.Z` (e.g. `release/v1.0.0`).
2. Update the CHANGELOG.md with the release notes for the new version. (We have a `mage` task to help with this in the `build` directory.)
3. Commit the changes and push the branch to GitHub.
4. Create a pull request from the release branch to `main`.
5. Once the pull request is merged, create a new tag on `main` with the version number (e.g. `v1.0.0`).
6. Push the tag to GitHub.
7. CI will build and publish the release to GitHub and Docker Hub.

### Snapshot Releases

Snapshot releases are created on-demand and are used to test the build and release process for stable releases.

To create a snapshot release, we run the [Snapshot Release GitHub Action](.github/workflows/snapshot.yml) workflow manually from the GitHub Actions UI.

The snapshot release does not create an actual release, it simply builds the binaries and uploads them as artifacts to the workflow run. The artifacts can then be downloaded and tested.

### Nightly Releases

Nightly releases are created automatically every night at 23:30 UTC via the [Nightly Release GitHub Action](.github/workflows/nightly.yml).

The nightly release also does not create an actual release. Unlike the snapshot release, the nightly release does not upload the binaries as artifacts. Instead, the nightly release builds a Docker image and pushes it to Docker Hub and GitHub Container Registry (Linux arm and x64 only). The Docker image is tagged with the version number (e.g. `v1.0.0-nightly`).

Currently the nightly release only builds the Docker image for Linux. We do not build a nightly release for macOS.

    13→
    14→// TracingConfig contains fields, which configure tracing telemetry
    15→// output destinations.
    16→type TracingConfig struct {
    17→	Enabled       bool                `json:"enabled" mapstructure:"enabled" yaml:"enabled"`
    18→	Exporter      TracingExporter     `json:"exporter,omitempty" mapstructure:"exporter" yaml:"exporter,omitempty"`
    19→	Propagators   []TracingPropagator `json:"propagators,omitempty" mapstructure:"propagators" yaml:"propagators,omitempty"`
    20→	SamplingRatio float64             `json:"samplingRatio,omitempty" mapstructure:"samplingRatio" yaml:"samplingRatio,omitempty"`
    21→	Jaeger        JaegerTracingConfig `json:"jaeger,omitempty" mapstructure:"jaeger" yaml:"jaeger,omitempty"`
    22→	Zipkin        ZipkinTracingConfig `json:"zipkin,omitempty" mapstructure:"zipkin" yaml:"zipkin,omitempty"`
    23→	OTLP          OTLPTracingConfig   `json:"otlp,omitempty" mapstructure:"otlp" yaml:"otlp,omitempty"`
    24→}

    27→	v.SetDefault("tracing", map[string]any{

1→package tracing
     2→
     3→import (
     4→	"context"
     5→	"fmt"
     6→	"net/url"
     7→	"strconv"
     8→	"sync"
     9→
    10→	"go.flipt.io/flipt/internal/config"
    11→	"go.opentelemetry.io/otel/exporters/jaeger"
    12→	"go.opentelemetry.io/otel/exporters/otlp/otlptrace"
    13→	"go.opentelemetry.io/otel/exporters/otlp/otlptrace/otlptracegrpc"
    14→	"go.opentelemetry.io/otel/exporters/otlp/otlptrace/otlptracehttp"
    15→	"go.opentelemetry.io/otel/exporters/zipkin"
    16→	"go.opentelemetry.io/otel/sdk/resource"
    17→	tracesdk "go.opentelemetry.io/otel/sdk/trace"
    18→	semconv "go.opentelemetry.io/otel/semconv/v1.24.0"
    19→)
    20→
    21→// newResource constructs a trace resource with Flipt-specific attributes.
    22→// It incorporates schema URL, service name, service version, and OTLP environment data
    23→func newResource(ctx context.Context, fliptVersion string) (*resource.Resource, error) {
    24→	return resource.New(
    25→		ctx,
    26→		resource.WithSchemaURL(semconv.SchemaURL),
    27→		resource.WithAttributes(
    28→			semconv.ServiceName("flipt"),
    29→			semconv.ServiceVersion(fliptVersion),
    30→		),
    31→		resource.WithFromEnv(),
    32→		resource.WithTelemetrySDK(),
    33→		resource.WithContainer(),
    34→		resource.WithHost(),
    35→		resource.WithProcessRuntimeVersion(),
    36→		resource.WithProcessRuntimeName(),
    37→		resource.WithProcessRuntimeDescription(),
    38→	)
    39→}
    40→
    41→// NewProvider creates a new TracerProvider configured for Flipt tracing.
    42→func NewProvider(ctx context.Context, fliptVersion string, cfg config.TracingConfig) (*tracesdk.TracerProvider, error) {
    43→	traceResource, err := newResource(ctx, fliptVersion)
    44→	if err != nil {
    45→		return nil, err
    46→	}
    47→
    48→	return tracesdk.NewTracerProvider(
    49→		tracesdk.WithResource(traceResource),
    50→		tracesdk.WithSampler(tracesdk.TraceIDRatioBased(cfg.SamplingRatio)),
    51→	), nil
    52→}
    53→
    54→var (
    55→	traceExpOnce sync.Once
    56→	traceExp     tracesdk.SpanExporter
    57→	traceExpFunc func(context.Context) error = func(context.Context) error { return nil }
    58→	traceExpErr  error
    59→)
    60→
    61→// GetExporter retrieves a configured tracesdk.SpanExporter based on the provided configuration.
    62→// Supports Jaeger, Zipkin and OTLP
    63→func GetExporter(ctx context.Context, cfg *config.TracingConfig) (tracesdk.SpanExporter, func(context.Context) error, error) {
    64→	traceExpOnce.Do(func() {
    65→		switch cfg.Exporter {
    66→		case config.TracingJaeger:
    67→			traceExp, traceExpErr = jaeger.New(jaeger.WithAgentEndpoint(
    68→				jaeger.WithAgentHost(cfg.Jaeger.Host),
    69→				jaeger.WithAgentPort(strconv.FormatInt(int64(cfg.Jaeger.Port), 10)),
    70→			))
    71→		case config.TracingZipkin:
    72→			traceExp, traceExpErr = zipkin.New(cfg.Zipkin.Endpoint)
    73→		case config.TracingOTLP:
    74→			u, err := url.Parse(cfg.OTLP.Endpoint)
    75→			if err != nil {
    76→				traceExpErr = fmt.Errorf("parsing otlp endpoint: %w", err)
    77→				return
    78→			}
    79→
    80→			var client otlptrace.Client
    81→			switch u.Scheme {
    82→			case "http", "https":
    83→				client = otlptracehttp.NewClient(
    84→					otlptracehttp.WithEndpoint(u.Host+u.Path),
    85→					otlptracehttp.WithHeaders(cfg.OTLP.Headers),
    86→				)
    87→			case "grpc":
    88→				// TODO: support additional configuration options
    89→				client = otlptracegrpc.NewClient(
    90→					otlptracegrpc.WithEndpoint(u.Host+u.Path),
    91→					otlptracegrpc.WithHeaders(cfg.OTLP.Headers),
    92→					// TODO: support TLS
    93→					otlptracegrpc.WithInsecure(),
    94→				)
    95→			default:
    96→				// because of url parsing ambiguity, we'll assume that the endpoint is a host:port with no scheme
    97→				client = otlptracegrpc.NewClient(
    98→					otlptracegrpc.WithEndpoint(cfg.OTLP.Endpoint),
    99→					otlptracegrpc.WithHeaders(cfg.OTLP.Headers),
   100→					// TODO: support TLS
   101→					otlptracegrpc.WithInsecure(),
   102→				)
   103→			}
   104→
   105→			traceExp, traceExpErr = otlptrace.New(ctx, client)
   106→			traceExpFunc = func(ctx context.Context) error {
   107→				return traceExp.Shutdown(ctx)
   108→			}
   109→
   110→		default:
   111→			traceExpErr = fmt.Errorf("unsupported tracing exporter: %s", cfg.Exporter)
   112→			return
   113→		}
   114→	})
   115→
   116→	return traceExp, traceExpFunc, traceExpErr
   117→}
   118→

    64→
    65→type grpcRegister interface {
    66→	RegisterGRPC(*grpc.Server)
    67→}
    68→
    69→type grpcRegisterers []grpcRegister
    70→
    71→func (g *grpcRegisterers) Add(r grpcRegister) {
    72→	*g = append(*g, r)
    73→}
    74→
    75→func (g grpcRegisterers) RegisterGRPC(s *grpc.Server) {
    76→	for _, register := range g {
    77→		register.RegisterGRPC(s)
    78→	}
    79→}
    80→
    81→// GRPCServer configures the dependencies associated with the Flipt GRPC Service.
    82→// It provides an entrypoint to start serving the gRPC stack (Run()).
    83→// Along with a teardown function (Shutdown(ctx)).
    84→type GRPCServer struct {
    85→	*grpc.Server
    86→
    87→	logger *zap.Logger
    88→	cfg    *config.Config
    89→	ln     net.Listener
    90→
    91→	shutdownFuncs []func(context.Context) error
    92→}
    93→
    94→// NewGRPCServer constructs the core Flipt gRPC service including its dependencies
    95→// (e.g. tracing, metrics, storage, migrations, caching and cleanup).
    96→// It returns an instance of *GRPCServer which callers can Run().
    97→func NewGRPCServer(
    98→	ctx context.Context,
    99→	logger *zap.Logger,
   100→	cfg *config.Config,
   101→	info info.Flipt,
   102→	forceMigrate bool,
   103→) (*GRPCServer, error) {
   104→	logger = logger.With(zap.String("server", "grpc"))
   105→	server := &GRPCServer{
   106→		logger: logger,
   107→		cfg:    cfg,
   108→	}
   109→
   110→	var err error
   111→	server.ln, err = net.Listen("tcp", fmt.Sprintf("%s:%d", cfg.Server.Host, cfg.Server.GRPCPort))
   112→	if err != nil {
   113→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("creating grpc listener: %w", err)
   114→	}
   115→
   116→	server.onShutdown(func(context.Context) error {
   117→		return server.ln.Close()
   118→	})
   119→
   120→	var store storage.Store
   121→
   122→	switch cfg.Storage.Type {
   123→	case "", config.DatabaseStorageType:
   124→		db, builder, driver, dbShutdown, err := getDB(ctx, logger, cfg, forceMigrate)
   125→		if err != nil {
   126→			return nil, err
   127→		}
   128→
   129→		server.onShutdown(dbShutdown)
   130→
   131→		switch driver {
   132→		case fliptsql.SQLite, fliptsql.LibSQL:
   133→			store = sqlite.NewStore(db, builder, logger)

arguments.content:
# Flipt Release Notes

## Overview

This release introduces significant improvements to OpenTelemetry (OTEL) tracing instrumentation, providing better observability and more granular control over trace sampling and propagation.

## Breaking Changes

None. This is a backward-compatible release.

## New Features

### Tracing Configuration Enhancements

#### Sampling Ratio Configuration
- **`tracing.samplingRatio`**: New configuration option to control the fraction of traces to sample.
  - Type: `number` (float64)
  - Range: `0.0` to `1.0`
  - Default: `1` (sample 100% of traces)
  - Example configuration:
    ```yaml
    tracing:
      enabled: true
      exporter: jaeger
      samplingRatio: 0.1  # Sample 10% of traces
    ```
  - **Migration Path**: If you upgrade from a previous version and want to reduce trace overhead, set this value to less than 1. For example, `0.1` will sample only 10% of traces.

#### Trace Propagators Configuration
- **`tracing.propagators`**: New configuration option to specify OpenTelemetry text map propagators for distributed tracing.
  - Type: `array` of strings
  - Default: `["tracecontext", "baggage"]`
  - Supported values:
    - `tracecontext` - W3C Trace Context propagator
    - `baggage` - W3C Baggage propagator
    - `b3` - Zipkin B3 Single propagator
    - `b3multi` - Zipkin B3 Multi propagator
    - `jaeger` - Jaeger propagator
    - `xray` - AWS X-Ray propagator
    - `ottrace` - OpenTracing propagator
    - `none` - No-op propagator
  - Example configuration:
    ```yaml
    tracing:
      enabled: true
      exporter: jaeger
      propagators:
        - tracecontext
        - baggage
        - jaeger  # Add Jaeger propagation support
    ```
  - **Use Case**: Enable multiple propagators when your traces need to be compatible with different trace collection systems or when propagating traces across systems using different standards.

### Enhanced Tracing Resource Information

Tracing spans now include more semantic information from OpenTelemetry semantic conventions v1.24.0:
- **Service Information**: Service name (`flipt`) and version
- **Container Information**: Container ID, image name, and other container metadata (when running in containers)
- **Host Information**: Hostname, OS type, and architecture
- **Runtime Information**: Go runtime version, runtime name, and description
- **Telemetry SDK Information**: Information about the OpenTelemetry SDK version being used
- **Environment Variables**: Automatic detection of OTEL environment variables

## Configuration Guide

### Sampling Configuration Example

To reduce trace overhead in production while maintaining high sampling in development:

```yaml
version: "1.0"

tracing:
  enabled: true
  exporter: jaeger
  jaeger:
    host: localhost
    port: 6831
  # Sample only 10% of traces in production
  samplingRatio: 0.1
  # Use standard W3C propagators
  propagators:
    - tracecontext
    - baggage
```

### Multi-Propagator Configuration Example

For integrations with multiple observability systems:

```yaml
tracing:
  enabled: true
  exporter: otlp
  otlp:
    endpoint: localhost:4317
  # Support multiple trace formats
  propagators:
    - tracecontext    # W3C standard
    - baggage         # W3C baggage
    - jaeger          # Jaeger format
    - b3multi         # Zipkin format
```

## Configuration Validation

The configuration now validates:
- **Sampling Ratio**: Must be a number between 0 and 1 (inclusive)
- **Propagators**: Must be valid propagator names from the supported list

Invalid configurations will result in a clear error message at startup.

## Upgrade Guide

### For Users Not Using Tracing

No action required. The tracing configuration defaults remain backward compatible.

### For Users With Tracing Enabled

1. **Optional**: If you want to reduce trace overhead in production, add the `samplingRatio` option:
   ```yaml
   tracing:
     enabled: true
     samplingRatio: 0.1  # Adjust based on your needs
   ```

2. **Optional**: If you need to support trace propagation between systems, configure multiple propagators:
   ```yaml
   tracing:
     enabled: true
     propagators:
       - tracecontext
       - baggage
       - jaeger  # Add if integrating with Jaeger systems
   ```

3. **Restart** your Flipt instance after configuration changes.

### Verification

After upgrading, verify tracing is working:
1. Check Flipt startup logs for any tracing configuration errors
2. Confirm traces are reaching your trace collector
3. If sampling is configured, verify that the expected percentage of traces are being collected

## Bug Fixes

- Fixed tracing configuration tests to properly validate new propagator and sampling ratio options
- Corrected trace resource construction to use latest OpenTelemetry semantic conventions

## Dependencies

- Updated OpenTelemetry semantic conventions to v1.24.0
- Added support for OpenTelemetry contrib propagators auto-detection (`autoprop`)

## API Changes

### gRPC/REST APIs

No changes to the public gRPC or REST APIs. This release only enhances observability through improved tracing configuration.

### Configuration Schema

New fields added to `tracing` configuration:
- `samplingRatio` (optional, default: 1)
- `propagators` (optional, default: ["tracecontext", "baggage"])

Full schema validation is available in `config/flipt.schema.json`.

## Performance Considerations

- **Sampling**: Setting `samplingRatio` to less than 1.0 can significantly reduce trace collection overhead in high-traffic deployments
- **Propagators**: Adding more propagators has minimal performance impact (evaluated at request time)
- **Resource Attributes**: Automatic resource collection may add small overhead but provides valuable context in traces

## Troubleshooting

### Traces Not Being Collected

- Verify `tracing.enabled: true` in configuration
- Check that the trace exporter endpoint is reachable
- Ensure `samplingRatio` is not set to 0
- Review Flipt logs for tracing initialization errors

### Invalid Propagator Error

- Verify propagator names match the supported list exactly
- Common mistake: `trace-context` instead of `tracecontext`
- Check configuration file syntax (YAML indentation)

### High Trace Volume

- Reduce `samplingRatio` to sample fewer traces
- Example: `samplingRatio: 0.01` samples 1% of traces

## Questions or Issues?

If you encounter any issues with this release:
1. Check the [Flipt documentation](https://www.flipt.io/docs)
2. Review [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/flipt-io/flipt/issues)
3. Open a new issue with configuration details and error messages

  - Supported values:
    - `tracecontext` - W3C Trace Context propagator
    - `baggage` - W3C Baggage propagator
    - `b3` - Zipkin B3 Single propagator
    - `b3multi` - Zipkin B3 Multi propagator
    - `jaeger` - Jaeger propagator
    - `xray` - AWS X-Ray propagator
    - `ottrace` - OpenTracing propagator
    - `none` - No-op propagator
  - Example configuration:
    ```yaml
    tracing:
      enabled: true
      exporter: jaeger
      propagators:
        - tracecontext
        - baggage
        - jaeger  # Add Jaeger propagation support
    ```
  - **Use Case**: Enable multiple propagators when your traces need to be compatible with different trace collection systems or when propagating traces across systems using different standards.

### Enhanced Tracing Resource Information

Tracing spans now include more semantic information from OpenTelemetry semantic conventions v1.24.0:
- **Service Information**: Service name (`flipt`) and version
- **Container Information**: Container ID, image name, and other container metadata (when running in containers)
- **Host Information**: Hostname, OS type, and architecture
- **Runtime Information**: Go runtime version, runtime name, and description
- **Telemetry SDK Information**: Information about the OpenTelemetry SDK version being used
- **Environment Variables**: Automatic detection of OTEL environment variables

## Configuration Guide

### Sampling Configuration Example

To reduce trace overhead in production while maintaining high sampling in development:

```yaml
version: "1.0"

tracing:
  enabled: true
  exporter: jaeger
  jaeger:
    host: localhost
    port: 6831
  # Sample only 10% of traces in production
  samplingRatio: 0.1
  # Use standard W3C propagators
  propagators:
    - tracecontext
    - baggage
```

### Multi-Propagator Configuration Example

For integrations with multiple observability systems:

```yaml
tracing:
  enabled: true
  exporter: otlp
  otlp:
    endpoint: localhost:4317
  # Support multiple trace formats
  propagators:
    - tracecontext    # W3C standard
    - baggage         # W3C baggage
    - jaeger          # Jaeger format
    - b3multi         # Zipkin format
```

## Configuration Validation

The configuration now validates:
- **Sampling Ratio**: Must be a number between 0 and 1 (inclusive)
- **Propagators**: Must be valid propagator names from the supported list

Invalid configurations will result in a clear error message at startup.

## Upgrade Guide

### For Users Not Using Tracing

No action required. The tracing configuration defaults remain backward compatible.

### For Users With Tracing Enabled

1. **Optional**: If you want to reduce trace overhead in production, add the `samplingRatio` option:
   ```yaml
   tracing:
     enabled: true
     samplingRatio: 0.1  # Adjust based on your needs
   ```

2. **Optional**: If you need to support trace propagation between systems, configure multiple propagators:
   ```yaml
   tracing:
     enabled: true
     propagators:
       - tracecontext
       - baggage
       - jaeger  # Add if integrating with Jaeger systems
   ```

3. **Restart** your Flipt instance after configuration changes.

### Verification

After upgrading, verify tracing is working:
1. Check Flipt startup logs for any tracing configuration errors
2. Confirm traces are reaching your trace collector
3. If sampling is configured, verify that the expected percentage of traces are being collected

## Bug Fixes

- Fixed tracing configuration tests to properly validate new propagator and sampling ratio options
- Corrected trace resource construction to use latest OpenTelemetry semantic conventions

## Dependencies

- Updated OpenTelemetry semantic conventions to v1.24.0
- Added support for OpenTelemetry contrib propagators auto-detection (`autoprop`)

## API Changes

### gRPC/REST APIs

No changes to the public gRPC or REST APIs. This release only enhances observability through improved tracing configuration.

### Configuration Schema

New fields added to `tracing` configuration:
- `samplingRatio` (optional, default: 1)
- `propagators` (optional, default: ["tracecontext", "baggage"])

Full schema validation is available in `config/flipt.schema.json`.

## Performance Considerations

- **Sampling**: Setting `samplingRatio` to less than 1.0 can significantly reduce trace collection overhead in high-traffic deployments
- **Propagators**: Adding more propagators has minimal performance impact (evaluated at request time)
- **Resource Attributes**: Automatic resource collection may add small overhead but provides valuable context in traces

## Troubleshooting

### Traces Not Being Collected

- Verify `tracing.enabled: true` in configuration
- Check that the trace exporter endpoint is reachable
- Ensure `samplingRatio` is not set to 0
- Review Flipt logs for tracing initialization errors

### Invalid Propagator Error

- Verify propagator names match the supported list exactly
- Common mistake: `trace-context` instead of `tracecontext`
- Check configuration file syntax (YAML indentation)

### High Trace Volume

- Reduce `samplingRatio` to sample fewer traces
- Example: `samplingRatio: 0.01` samples 1% of traces

## Questions or Issues?

If you encounter any issues with this release:
1. Check the [Flipt documentation](https://www.flipt.io/docs)
2. Review [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/flipt-io/flipt/issues)
3. Open a new issue with configuration details and error messages

# Flipt Release Notes

## Overview

This release introduces significant improvements to OpenTelemetry (OTEL) tracing instrumentation, providing better observability and more granular control over trace sampling and propagation.

## Breaking Changes

None. This is a backward-compatible release.

## New Features

### Tracing Configuration Enhancements

#### Sampling Ratio Configuration
- **`tracing.samplingRatio`**: New configuration option to control the fraction of traces to sample.
  - Type: `number` (float64)
  - Range: `0.0` to `1.0`
  - Default: `1` (sample 100% of traces)
  - Example configuration:
    ```yaml
    tracing:
      enabled: true
      exporter: jaeger
      samplingRatio: 0.1  # Sample 10% of traces
    ```
  - **Migration Path**: If you upgrade from a previous version and want to reduce trace overhead, set this value to less than 1. For example, `0.1` will sample only 10% of traces.

#### Trace Propagators Configuration
- **`tracing.propagators`**: New configuration option to specify OpenTelemetry text map propagators for distributed tracing.
  - Type: `array` of strings
  - Default: `["tracecontext", "baggage"]`
  - Supported values:
    - `tracecontext` - W3C Trace Context propagator
    - `baggage` - W3C Baggage propagator
    - `b3` - Zipkin B3 Single propagator
    - `b3multi` - Zipkin B3 Multi propagator
    - `jaeger` - Jaeger propagator
    - `xray` - AWS X-Ray propagator
    - `ottrace` - OpenTracing propagator
    - `none` - No-op propagator
  - Example configuration:
    ```yaml
    tracing:
      enabled: true
      exporter: jaeger
      propagators:
        - tracecontext
        - baggage
        - jaeger  # Add Jaeger propagation support
    ```
  - **Use Case**: Enable multiple propagators when your traces need to be compatible with different trace collection systems or when propagating traces across systems using different standards.

### Enhanced Tracing Resource Information

- **Telemetry SDK Information**: Information about the OpenTelemetry SDK version being used
- **Environment Variables**: Automatic detection of OTEL environment variables

```yaml
  enabled: true

    host: localhost
    port: 6831

  # Use standard W3C propagators
  propagators:
    - tracecontext
    - baggage
```

### Multi-Propagator Configuration Example

For integrations with multiple observability systems:

```yaml

  enabled: true
  exporter: otlp
  otlp:
    endpoint: localhost:4317

    - tracecontext    # W3C standard
    - baggage         # W3C baggage
    - jaeger          # Jaeger format
    - b3multi         # Zipkin format
```

   ```yaml
     enabled: true
   ```

   ```yaml
     enabled: true
   ```

3. **Restart** your Flipt instance after configuration changes.

3. If sampling is configured, verify that the expected percentage of traces are being collected

## Dependencies
